Dreams · Symbols · Numbers

Octopus Symbolism & Meaning

Of all the creatures that have entered the modern symbolic imagination, the octopus is among the strangest — a mind that does not seem to live where minds are supposed to live. It is often interpreted as an emblem of distributed intelligence, adaptive genius, and the capacity to engage many fronts at once, though the older traditions read it rather differently.

The core reading: a mind that is not one mind

The most consistent contemporary reading of the octopus is that it symbolises intelligence in unfamiliar form. Where the lion stands for sovereign power and the owl for a particular flavour of nocturnal wisdom, the octopus has come to represent a cognition that does not centralise — a way of being aware that proceeds through many limbs, many simultaneous experiments, many small autonomous decisions. The biology underwrites the symbol: roughly two-thirds of an octopus's neurons sit outside its central brain, distributed through its arms, which means each limb can taste, decide, and act with a degree of independence.

This makes the octopus an unusual symbolic resource. It is one of the few animal symbols that gives us an image of mind without a single throne — without the executive ego sitting at the centre dispatching orders. When the octopus appears in dreams or in conscious symbolic work, it is often read as touching on parts of the psyche that operate beneath or alongside the central self: the body's own knowing, the intuitive feelers that taste before they think, the parallel processes running while the surface mind is occupied elsewhere.

Many readers also take the octopus as a symbol of adaptability under pressure. Its body has almost no fixed shape; it can pour itself through any opening larger than its beak; it changes colour and texture in milliseconds. As a symbol, this tends to read as the psyche's capacity to reshape itself when conditions demand — neither rigid resistance nor collapse, but fluid reconfiguration around the obstacle.

The octopus across traditions

The older symbolic record is more ambivalent than the contemporary one. In ancient Greek and Mediterranean imagery, the octopus appears frequently on Minoan pottery from Crete, particularly the famous Marine Style vessels of the second millennium BCE, where its spiralling arms became one of the most elegant decorative motifs of the Bronze Age. Here it is associated with the sea's abundance and with the supple, encircling beauty of marine life, rather than threat.

Other traditions cast it darker. In Japanese folklore the octopus has a complex life: sometimes a comic figure, sometimes the akkorokamui, a giant red octopus-spirit of Ainu tradition associated with the waters around Hokkaido — both feared and venerated, asked for help with healing. Hokusai's famous nineteenth-century print of an octopus entwined with a woman cemented a more erotic and unsettling register in Western perception of the creature. In Hawaiian creation accounts preserved in the Kumulipo, the octopus (he'e) appears as a survivor from an earlier world, a creature carrying knowledge from a previous age.

Norse seafaring tradition gave us the kraken, often imagined as an enormous octopus or squid, a symbol of the unknown depth that could rise from below and undo a ship. This is the octopus as oceanic shadow — what lives beneath the waterline of consciousness and occasionally surfaces. Christian and early modern European symbolism inherited some of this register, often using many-armed sea creatures to figure greed, encompassment, or the corrupting reach of empire and monopoly. The political cartoon tradition still draws on this lineage when it depicts a power as an octopus whose tentacles reach into every domain.

Polynesian and Pacific Northwest indigenous traditions tend to honour the octopus more directly as a being of intelligence and skill. In some Haida and Tlingit stories the octopus is a powerful figure of the intertidal zone, a knower of the boundary between worlds. These readings have arguably aged better than the European ones, and they sit closer to what modern science now suggests about the creature itself.

A depth-psychological reading

Jung's framework gives the octopus a natural place. The sea is one of his most consistent images for the unconscious, and creatures that emerge from it tend to be read as contents rising from below the threshold of awareness. An octopus, specifically, often figures what Jung might have called an autonomous complex — a part of the psyche that has its own intelligence, its own appetites, and is not under the ego's direct command. The many arms can be read as the many ways such a complex reaches into a life: through habits, attractions, repeated patterns that seem to act independently of conscious decision.

Read more generously, the octopus can also figure the Self in a non-traditional mode — not the radiant mandala-centre, but an image of wholeness as distributed coordination, where the centre is everywhere and nowhere. For people whose individuation does not follow a tidy hierarchical model, this is sometimes a more accurate image of inner integration than the more classical ones.

Variations

A friendly or curious octopus. Often read as the psyche making contact with its own non-verbal intelligence — body wisdom, intuition, the part that knows by feel rather than by argument.

An octopus wrapping its arms around you. Tends to appear when someone feels entangled — by obligations, by a relationship, by a job that has grown too many connections to leave easily. The reading is usually less about external threat than about the question of whether the entanglement is welcome.

Interpretations on PsySymbol are reflective tools, not predictions or clinical advice. If a recurring symbol is genuinely affecting your sleep, mood, or wellbeing, a therapist will help more directly than any symbolic reading. See our methodology.
